title: ADVANCED ANATOMY ESSENTIALS: Myomodulation, vectors, biomechanics explained [Aesthetic Mastery Show]
filename: ADVANCED ANATOMY ESSENTIALS： Myomodulation, vectors, biomechanics explained [Aesthetic Mastery Show].en.vtt
excerpt: I have actually got some pictures of you from a treatment you had a long time ago that maybe we could share where you can see that your top lip is moving less in the after picture so that ability to raise your lip up is stronger before you had treatment and obviously put it put a…

segments.text: i have actually got some pictures of you from a treatment you had a long time ago that maybe we could share where you can see that your top lip is moving less in the after picture so that ability to raise your lip up is stronger before you had treatment and obviously put it put a product in and it resists some of that movement and it just looks slightly more harmonious welcome to the ascetics mastery show i'm dr tim pearce hi i'm miranda pierce and today we're bringing it all together muscles fat pads ligaments bone they are all altered by the injections that we give and we need to understand the complex dynamics between these structures when we inject so we can give our patients the excellent guidance that they need and get really top-notch advice to them in a way that they can understand it and then get beautiful results so in order to do this we have to look at the face from simple to complicated from that first understanding that you may have had when you first started injecting or

even before injecting to where you need to get to understand the subtle nuances of the human form so let's dive in so if i'm an injector watching this now and i'm relatively new into the game how am i understanding the face well i think what it starts with is with toxin treatments and if you think about the muscles from that perspective the first understanding you get is if you relax the muscle then the lines in the face start to fade and i remember being kind of amazed by that when i first discovered it which is all the lines and wrinkles are actually all the time fading it's just that we keep waking them up by moving and if you stop the movement the lines fade and that was my first very simple understanding of how the muscles affected our appearance but thankfully there's a lot more to that because otherwise i would have got quite bored quite soon so there's a lot more to understand but that was step one and what's the next level how do you take it up a notch well once you've understood the relaxation component you soon will stumble across the idea that there's actually

some relationship between different muscles so there are vectors across the face a vector is a force with a particular direction and all of your facial features are hol are held in place by multiple different forces and the muscles and gravity are the big components of the forces that control the position and but you start to realize that there's this tug of war going on and that the position of your eyebrow and maybe the corner of your mouth your cheek all of those structures are held in balance by these different forces gravity the attachment of the muscles the strength of the muscles and of course there's more to it that we're going to get to as well give me an example of where gravity impacts ascetics well this is the first step after realizing you can relax the muscle is that you realize if you over relax a muscle that gravity will take over and you get a drop and those are some of the first side effects most new practitioners will experience will be maybe the loss of an eyebrow arch some flattening to the brow if you're very lucky of true brow ptosis and all of these things um hinted the

fact that there's a force involved here that the muscle was resisting which is gravity and that's the first understanding we need that you can't simply relax muscles and hope that lines will disappear without them being a possible down downside literally and so we need to then try not to over treat muscles so there's enough muscle tension left to support structures so how about these vectors give us an example make that real for us so yeah i do think a lot of people don't immediately gel with that word because a vector is a it's a term from physics it's force and direction so every muscle in your face has it has the ability to impose a force on the skin or on a feature and then it also has a direction in which it pulls so that combination of force and direction will tell you where the resting position of a structure is so eyebrow is the best example if you think about your eyebrow it is held in position by four different muscles you have a corrugator muscle that pulls medially you have the frontalis muscle that pulls superiorly you have orbicularis oculi that pulls inferiorly all of those muscles are

imposing a resting tension there are also some ligaments and fat pads and obviously the elasticity in the skin is also involved but for now just think about the muscles because that's what we alter with toxin this little battle is going on what we effectively do with a muscle relaxant is you weaken one side of this tug of war and then the other muscles pull in a different direction and what makes it even more complex is that we have movement in different expressions so in different expressions you'll see different the vectors play out in different ways because the net effect the overall forces involved are being altered when you relax so um some of the over treatment area over treated signs of toxin are when these vectors are out of play can we change the back do we change the vectors yes you cannot relax a muscle without altering the vector across the face so you are altering the forces across the surface of the skin otherwise there's no point treating it and usually this is hopefully a good thing but sometimes you get these over treated or you know botox signed type looks and

that's when the vectors are slightly disrupted in a way that our brains can pick up very subtly that there's something unnatural here and that's when you have a vector that is simply just an unnatural vector you'd see across the face would an example be where you have those little like rainbow lines that come just above yeah so if you overlift an eyebrow probably the mo the one people will resonate the most with will be spock brow because then you've got a depression in the middle and an over extension over uh lift it laterally so that sign medial depression lateral elevation is a disruption of the vectors that's so significant that we don't think it looks natural but there are many examples you sometimes see it if you look closely at some patients maybe in the age 45 slim who have orbicularis oculi treated so they're just having their crow's feet treated when they smile they get a slightly lower high point on their cheek and they just don't look it's not like a glowing smile anymore it's slightly depressed that's a very subtle vector that you can look for as well

but every area that you treat look out for the vectors think about what the net effect on the collection of muscles affecting that area will be and then actually look for it in your after photos and you'll see these little shifts and with time obviously nearly all of them should be good shifts you know little lifts that we like but you will spot also ones that are not that good um another one that's quite common in terms of toxin side effects is when you treat it a lot laterally so orbicular soccer life round forehead you get this medial pull so when people smile it's not as opposed by orbicularis oculi so they get this kind of pinched look medially and that's basically because all the muscles are pulling in that direction and the ones that were resisting that force are now relaxed so you don't see it at rest the before and after pictures look brilliant but the dynamics of the face are subtly affected in a way that people just so should probably just have botox it's not necessarily ugly but it's a difference that you can see what's next after vectors so the next thing that i learned after

vectors was this idea of biomechanics and i actually learned this because um i have one leg that's slightly shorter than the other only by a centimeter um and i had a physiotherapist do a really good assessment of me and it was one of those experiences where you think wow you know i've never thought of it like this before and you know she was looking at how much my muscles were extending in different places and what my flexibility was like and explaining why this was causing back pain this whole complex narrative which he put together explained it beautifully and i got it and i was like wow this is a complex system and the same thing applies in the face which is there are little differences between different people that amount to different results with the same treatment if you're not if you're trying to do a blanket approach for everyone so one example would be the insert origin and insertion points so the angle at which a muscle is attached from the bone onto the skin can change what the net effect is on when you relax that muscle so the corrugator is a really good example of this if you think about the origin and insertion of the

corrugator that can completely change whether you're likely to get a medial brow drop after if you just treat the frontalis so it's a common rule many people say is i never treat the frontalis without treating the glabellar complex or the corrugator and the reason for that is the majority of people have this downward vector so when they frown it pulls their eyebrows down not just together but not everyone's like that and if you look closely you'll find maybe 10 15 people where it's actually quite neutral so they don't have a downed vector they're just creating those elevenses and those people actually probably don't need to have their glabellar complex treated when they're having a toxin treatment but that comes from understanding the biomechanics so origin insertion angle changes the vectors across the face and you can see this also with the size of the muscle but it's happening in all the areas that we look at so the same might be true around the modiolus and how that's connected to the jawline and to the midface that you're going to get subtly different um results in terms of when that when the face is dynamic they just simply you get it for the same

muscle you get a slightly different movement that might impact on your treatment so you've got to understand the face in terms of those um as i say the biomechanics how can you diagnose someone has a different biomechanics someone else well you need to watch them moving so um it's very simple i actually the probably some of the best observation you make is when you're not asking them to move and you're just listening to them speak and another good example would be with non-surgical rhinoplasty it's always a good idea when you're talking to someone to watch if their nose is moving a lot so if you see someone who's got a curved nose and they've got a lot of movement so their depressor nasal muscle is powerfully pulling down the tip of their nose that is a diagnosis i make without asking them to move i observe it while i'm talking to them and it becomes one of the treatment options that i can deliver if they want a straighter nose to have a bit of toxin as well as the filler yeah well okay so um but that aside you should ask your patients to move and yeah the trick is to try and get a more natural movement because if you get patients who do special i call it the special

toxin testing phase it's not often not natural at all so you'll often new practitioners will often get people coming back for top-ups and saying i've still got movement and you ask them to do the movement for example frown and they do this and that's essentially using orbicularis oculi to recreate the frown muscle the frown line so they are they want to make sure they're getting good value for money they want to make sure they're getting a good result fair enough and they go into the mirror and they use every muscle they can possibly recruit to make sure that there's that they've tested everything well and they'll eventually get some movement and then they think they need a top up and the inexperienced injector will top them up and then wonder why things haven't changed and it's because it's not a natural face so when you assess the patient you should firstly educate them about that but also you yourself should try and get them to express natural movements so um little smile you know look a bit irritated look cross but don't but you don't have to do some extreme treatment um and that's a really helpful way of understanding their face how

about fat pads how are they affecting all these other mechanisms yeah so fat pads can help and hinder and in obviously different stages of life um they can be helpful or more hindering and also um that's the main thing we're actually trying to control with dermal fillers in my opinion that's one of the key things we're altering the first way that they interact with muscles is if you put on weight if you have extra volume they dampen movement so it's literally like having the suspension with some resistance in it so that as a muscle contracts it's now trying to squeeze fat pad and even as it relaxes there'll be other fat pads that are dampening the amount of movement it can make um so that's the most simple form of it now the other side of this is obviously if your fat pads shrink then you're also going to have more movement so you'll notice this in older people who don't put on weight in fact they lose volume that they become almost caricaturish so they get much more moving so a small smile becomes much bigger and you start to see basically a more exaggerated movement which is one of the subtle signs of

aging um actually i was watching tony blair on the tv recently and that's one of the things i noticed is you can almost see some of the characteristics that they might have done on tv years ago it's there's just that essence of it but it's true for all of us who maintain our weight as you just get more of those movements because you lose fat in your face if you maintain your weight anyway as you get older and that you just see more of that give us an example of that relationship between filler and the muscles at play well everywhere that you inject filler has this ability to dampen the muscles so ideally you if you're treating people where they've lost volume you'll see that in a good way so one example might be upper lip lines a little bit of dermal filler with where someone is pursing easily at rest and it might just relax that muscle slightly and they look more relaxed they look happier because they don't they're not they don't have that pursed lip which is sometimes associated with anger and but you'll see in cheeks you see in foreheads temples um all of them that there's a kind of harmony of youth when you have just the right amount of fat pads where you have movement but it's not too exaggerated

and all of the areas where you tend to inject filler tend to be resisted be resisting movement of some muscles and often that's a good way i have actually got some pictures of you from a treatment you had a long time ago that maybe we could share where you can see that your top lip is moving less in the after picture so that ability to raise your lip up is stronger before you had treatment and obviously put it put a product in and it resists some of that movement and it just looks slightly more harmonious there's another thing you can see on that picture which is eyebrow effect and movement around your eyes and it's a little bit like a as if you could do a very subtle toxin treatment that you just don't get as many eye lines and it's basically the stability brought about by stabilizing the cheek and the zygomatic muscles and their ability to move all that tissue superiorly when you smile any other way that fat affects the muscle well if you i think if you put on a lot of weight if you have too much fat in the face it actually dampens down expression and you can look a little bit less expressive than what would be most beautiful i certainly

noticed that a few times um and then there's also another benefit which is leverage so there are certain muscles that seem to sit on top of a fat pad and if you add the volume back underneath if it's been lost you may get a bit more lift it's very subtle and you know it's debatable but maybe some of the deeper fat pads in the frontalis and the um and fat pads in the frontalis and the um and the midface that if you add a bit of the midface that if you add a bit of volume you might notice a bit more support so they can be used to lift as well as dampen a muscle what would you be lifting in the case of the midface so the corner of the mouth is as obvious often the result um nasolabial fold may lift slightly um it's very subtle and it's certainly debatable and you don't and it depends on your injection technique i'm not saying that every time you inject the cheek that you'll lift the nasolabial fold or the mediolabial fold um but i've certainly seen in some cases that there's this synergistic effect where it seems to just create a nice positive lift coming back to that point around fat in the face i always thought

more fat in the face equals fewer wrinkles is that not what you're saying no i think that's actually broadly true that most of the fine lines and wrinkles are less and they're less because the muscle is less able to create a crease so you often have you'll often see someone who's lost weight develop fine lines and wrinkles but they also get definition at the same time and so that there is definitely good elements of being of having that extra volume but if it goes to the next level obviously there's more to actually support and you might develop needle labial fold nasolabial folds and these are caused by the fat pad descending and not being supported by the muscle she's like gravity again yeah gravity plus a fat pad yeah tell me about ligaments and how they come into play ligaments are obviously sporting the surface of the skin and we've got a whole video on that's worth watching um which we did previously but the key thing is that with the relationship with muscles is that as muscles contract they particularly when the fat pads have shrunk they tend to reveal the position of the ligaments because the ligament is the last thing resisting and you'll start to see shadows lines

and shadows caused by the ligaments hanging onto the skin as the muscles contracting so this is one of the reasons why during a strong contraction a strong expression you may see more complexity come out of the face so you see more detail more anatomy underneath effectively and a lot of that is the ligaments revealing their position through a little dip caused by the muscle contracting how about this maya modulation of hairy talking about so yeah my modulation's worth understanding you'll hear it come up at conferences and some clinicians like to debate whether it's such a big deal or not um it's certainly very interesting and it does make sense when you look at it from first principles so um everything we've been talking about ultimately is myomodulation if you do something to the face you'll modulate how the muscle interacts with the rest of the structures but there's a deeper level to understand it on you can think of myomodulation as like a tug of war within a tug of war and every muscle is actually made up of a tug of war so you've got myosin and actin working

together and i like to literally picture them like two teams in a tug of war except the difference is when you look at a sarcomere which is the unit of muscle the teams are switched to face the other direction and they're pulling the walls together um to get closer to pull to actually contract the muscle so if you think of this going on um the rope is actin and the players are the myosin and as they pull um they have a different amount of rope and a different amount of room left to play with so you can picture this first at the extremes if you think of a really stretched muscle there are very few players who actually have any rope to hold on to and the resting strength of that muscle the first ability for it to contract is quite low it's not very strong at the other end of the spectrum if you picture that muscle all contracted up there isn't actually any room to pull the rope any further and they're all compressed together and that's also a weak muscle so in between that is the ideal position of a muscle and that's when a muscle that kind of

sustains itself the best when there's a good amount of overlap and there's room to contract and many of those myosin and actin fibers are easily in contact and the resting tone is therefore at its best that's what it's like in youth and then we have different amounts of either over stretching or over compression which results in the features being negatively affected can we restore that balance point with filler i think you can in certain age groups at the right time with well-placed product you can create a shift in the right direction that has a disproportionately good effect and that's really what my modulation is all about which is if you have a muscle that's for example there's so little support from the fat pads that it's kind of stretched and then you replace that fat pad you will allow it to move back into its zone of strength and that will pull up the features slightly better and that's essentially what we're trying to do with well placed product all the time so in maybe someone in their 40s or something like that with not very much filler you could get a disproportionately good result is that what you're saying yeah if you

get people at the right age with the right with well-placed products it can have a really good result um now obviously the more fat pad that's lost and the more stretched the muscle is the harder it is to get those while results but there's definitely a sweet spot just like there is with a muscle where it's attainable this maya modulation you said there's a bit of debate around it how do people even discover it well the concept really comes from cardiology so there's an old law that most of us learn at uh when we're at university which is starling's law of the heart so in stalling's law of the heart has been discovered by physiologists that as you stretch the cardiac muscle initially it responds with a more powerful contraction so as the heart feels a bit more rapidly if you're doing exercise you squeeze it out more rapidly which is a self-balancing mechanism and there's an element of that with all muscles that as you know there's a sweet spot in which case it can respond better and that's the case with the heart um but as it goes beyond that point if you overstretch cardiac muscle it then

suddenly gets weaker but similarly if it's under stretched it's weaker so there's a sweet spot which in a heart you can measure beautifully because they simply just change the amount of volume coming in and measure what comes out and you can see that there's the sweet spot and that's exactly what we're talking about with my modulation in the face which is that every muscle has its sweet spot as it gets pushed out of its sweet spot it may resist that for a while and then it goes a little bit too far and there's a basically a degree of collapse that's the theory give us an example on the face well what you're seeing in an older face that's suffering from this lack of myomodulation is it tends to be a midface laxity and a lower face dominance so effectively you have a jowl you maybe have an upturn of the chin um and heaviness here with cheeks that are quite drawn and perhaps a bit more of the white of the eye showing so this is all it's all those emotional tones you get with the scent of tissue so sad tired and all of those elements unfortunately come from dissentive tissue caused by a lack of support

so if you get a good treatment just right and you support those structures you get this sense of lightness and lifting and obviously that's what the goal of many of our aesthetic treatments are from placing filler in a way that gets the muscle back its sweet spot yes absolutely and you'll see this we'll show that picture that i think you're exhibiting this exact effect which is that you get often get a little bit of a brow lift now if you replace volume around a cheek for example and you're often just supporting that muscle so that it can relax a little bit and you get a lift in the eyebrows for example drop us a comment below if you found that really useful and if you would like any more knowledge on the muscles tim has created a beautiful muscle poster that you can have for your clinic all so i'll drop the link in the description below thanks for watching don't forget to like and subscribe and we'll see you next week take you
